author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Chapter 46

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "In the first Psalm David separates the blessed from the impious, not indeed making them into parties, but excluding all the impious from holiness.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: ‘Blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly, nor standeth in the way of sinners.’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him who had strayed from the path of righteousness, so that he should perish, return to it again.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: ‘Nor sitteth in the seat of the scornful.’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: When he gives this warning, O ye miserable men, why do you sit in that seat?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: ‘But his delight is in the law of the Lord; and in His law doth he meditate day and night.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And he shall be like a tree planted by the rivers of water, that bringeth forth his fruit in his season: his leaf also shall not wither; and whatsoever he doeth shall prosper.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: The ungodly are not so: but are like the chaff which the wind driveth away.’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He blindeth their eyes, so that they should not see.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: ‘Therefore the ungodly shall not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the congregation of the righteous.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For the Lord knoweth the way of the righteous: but the way of the ungodly shall perish.’"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: Who is there in the Scriptures that would not distinguish between these two classes of men?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But you slanderously charge the corn with the offenses of the chaff; and being yourselves mere chaff, you boast yourselves to be the only corn.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But the true prophets declare that both these classes have been mingled together throughout the whole world, that is, throughout the whole corn-field of the Lord, until the winnowing which is to take place on the day of judgment.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But I advise you to read that first Psalm in the Greek version, and then you will not venture to reproach the whole world with being of the party of Macarius; because you will perhaps come to understand of what Macarius there is a party among all the saints, who throughout all nations are blessed in the seed of Abraham.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For what stands in our language as "Blessed is the man," is in Greek Μαχάριος ἀνήρ.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But that Macarius who offends you, if he is a bad man, neither belongs to this division, nor is to its prejudice.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if he is a good man, let him prove his own work, that he may have glory in himself alone, and not in another.
